_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Protrusion of the entire rectum outside the anal verge (full-thickness protrusion)
Mucus and/or stool discharge associated with altered bowel habits
Partial prolapse: protrusion between 1.25 and 3.75 cm outside the anal verge, usually mucosal
Complete/Total prolapse (procidentia): protrusion of the rectum for more than 3.7 cm outside the anal verge, often entire rectum, sometimes with peritoneal sac, often associated with prolapse uterus
Complaints of prolapse rectum, constipation, incontinence
Function of pudendal nerve and puborectalis nerve findings
Excessive mucus discharge causing irritation to the perianal skin
Some degree of incontinence of faeces and flatus, urgency, perianal soiling
Rectal examination: lax anal sphincter, wide gaping on straining

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings especially per rectal examination, indication for procedure, and planned line of management
Digital rectal examination (DRE) / sigmoidoscopy / fluoroscopic defecography / MRI / CT / USG abdomen/ Barium enema / Colonoscopy

claim.label: Detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) with treatment details

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned line of treatment, indication for procedure?
Is history & clinical examination suggestive of prolapse?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was clinical evaluation ± imaging indicative of surgery?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge?

common_queries: History & clinical examination not suggestive of prolapse.
Clinical notes missing detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned line of treatment, or indication for procedure.
Clinical evaluation not confirming the diagnosis.
Detailed procedure / operative notes missing or incomplete.
Clinical evaluation ± imaging not indicative of surgery.
Discharge summary with follow-up advice missing.

clinical_key_pointers: Protrusion of the entire rectum outside the anal verge is the feature of rectal prolapse.
This condition is common in children and elderly patients.
The symptoms of rectal procidentia include a history of mild abdominal discomfort, incomplete bowel evacuation, rectal mass, and mucus and/or stool discharge associated with altered bowel habits.
The most common sign is a full-thickness protrusion of the rectum though the anus, which may be intermittent.

Partial Prolapse causes in infants: undeveloped sacral curve; in children: secondary to habitual constipation; can follow an attack of whooping cough or excessive straining; can follow an attack of diarrhea resulting in loss of fat in the ischiorectal fossae; in adults (females mostly): due to Torn perineum caused by obstetric trauma.

Complete/Total Prolapse causes: common in elderly women who are multipara; excessive straining; defective collagen maturation; presence of deep rectovesical pouch and excessive mobility of the rectum (mesorectum); intussusception in the first stage, initiated by certain factors such as diarrhoea, constipation and disorder of the pelvic floor.
surgical_options: Safe procedure to correct with minimal morbidity and without mortality.
To cure or to improve incontinence.
Abdominal Procedures: Wells operation, Ripstein sling operation, Mesh rectopexy, Lahaut's operation.
Laparoscopic mesh rectopexy has become gold standard-fast recovery, less pain, short hospital stay.
Mesh rectopexy corrects/prevents prolapse but does not correct chronic constipation.
Mesh rectopexy with resection is ideal for patients with constipation or patients with a redundant sigmoid colon.
